text: Upon entering the abdomen, the surgeon identifies a defect in the posterior left diaphragm with the colon, omentum, and spleen visible within the chest cavity. The stomach is also identified and carefully reduced from the chest back into the abdomen. The surgeon uses electric cautery to release adhesions from the diaphragm and free the hernia edges, ensuring careful retraction of the omentum to avoid tearing vessels.

The surgeon measures the posterior lateral diaphragmatic defect, which is 5 cm by 7 cm. The defect is closed using O-tychron interrupted mattress sutures, with barred felt cut into 1 by 2 cm rectangles used as pledgets to reinforce the sutures.

A seven French drain is placed into the thoracic cavity before the defect is fully closed. The most posterior aspect of the defect is sutured close to the ribs using three separate interrupted sutures, which are pulled through the abdominal wall fascia and tied externally. A stab incision is made subxiphoid, and an endoclose device is used to secure the suture by pulling it through the incision. The exteriorized suture is tightened to create tension on the defect and tied down.

A 15 by 20 centimeter dual-sided barred mesh with an echo positioning system is selected to cover and reinforce the defect closure. The left lobe of the liver is mobilized by releasing its attachments to the midline. Additionally, the peritoneal reflection of the descending colon at the splenic flexure is mobilized down and out of the way to accommodate the mesh placement.

The surgeon introduces the mesh into the abdominal cavity through the twelfth port and positions it over the most posterior aspect of the defect. The mesh is secured to both the diaphragm and the abdominal wall circumferentially with a 2-0 V-lock suture. The central portion of the mesh is also fixated to the diaphragm closure using the same suture.
The final appearance of the hernia repair is shown, with the mesh securely in place and the defect closed. The surgical site is stable, and the surrounding tissues appear intact.
